video_title: MacroVoices #534 Dr. Pippa Malmgren: Superpower War or Superpower Hug?
gist: With the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed for ~3 months over Iran's 441 kg of 60%-enriched uranium, Malmgren frames the standoff as one cell of a US-China-Russia 'Rubik's cube' grand bargain and stays optimistic (energy shock accelerates a molecules-to-atoms nuclear transition), while Bianco and Townsend argue there is no forcing mechanism, drones have neutralized naval power, and $150-200 oil is plausible within 60 days if the strait stays shut.

themes.theme: Hormuz / Iran nuclear standoff and the deal dynamics

insights.claim: As of late May 2026 the Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed for ~3 months, running below 10% of normal traffic, with the dispute centered on Iran's refusal to surrender its 60%-enriched uranium.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend (host)
insights.so_what: A multi-month physical supply chokepoint is the dominant macro tail risk; traders should watch traffic-volume data, not headline 'deal imminent' noise.
insights.quote: the Straight of Hormuz is still effectively closed. A few dozen ships got through this week, but we're still well below 10% of normal traffic levels.

insights.claim: Neither Trump nor Iran has moved one inch on the central issue: Iran's Supreme Leader reiterated last Thursday the 60% uranium cannot leave Iran under any circumstance, so 'hours away from a deal' talk is propaganda.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend (host)
insights.so_what: The 'imminent deal' narrative driving oil down ~10% is unsupported; a long-vol or buy-the-dip-oil stance is favored over fading the risk.
insights.quote: All of this talk about how we're just hours away from a deal... It's all a bunch of It's propaganda. It's not real.

insights.claim: Iran is a 'threshold state' holding 441 kg of 60%-enriched uranium (enough for up to 9-10 bombs), having done ~99% of the separative work; going from 60% to 90% would take only a few weeks IF operable centrifuges survived Operation Midnight Hammer.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend (host)
insights.so_what: Reframes the binary 'do they have a weapon' debate: the enriched material, not weaponization, is the binding constraint that keeps the conflict unresolved.
insights.quote: Iran has done 99% of the enrichment activity necessary... getting it from 60% to 90% would only take a few weeks with operating centrifuges.

insights.claim: Malmgren argues the Iran conflict is not a state-to-state negotiation but a 'price negotiation' with leaderless IRGC stragglers competing to be the one who shakes hands with the US, making a deal more likely and incentivizing them to hold out for a higher bid.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest, geopolitical economist)
insights.so_what: If correct, resolution comes via cash buyouts plus episodic military threats rather than formal diplomacy — a messier, headline-driven path that sustains oil volatility.
insights.quote: we're not actually in a state negotiation anymore. We're in a price negotiation.

insights.claim: Malmgren's contrarian read: Trump wants physical possession of the uranium less to stop a bomb than to forensically prove its origin is American — material she says he believes was supplied to Iran under Clinton/Obama/Biden-era deals.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Implies the US may not accept any down-blending compromise and insists on custody, lengthening the standoff and the oil supply risk.
insights.quote: the real reason the president wants the uranium is because he wants to prove definitively the origin of that uranium... his belief is that it is American

insights.claim: Bianco says physically retrieving Iran's uranium could take until next year because it is buried under hundreds of feet of rock and would first require building a landing strip for heavy excavation equipment — so an MOU changes nothing.
insights.speaker: Jim Bianco (guest, Bianco Research founder)
insights.so_what: Removes the 'quick handover' resolution path markets are pricing; supports a structurally longer crisis and higher-for-longer oil.
insights.quote: the first step in getting the nuclear material might very well be to send in the engineers to build a landing strip... that might not be till next year.

insights.claim: Malmgren's master framework: the US, China and Russia are negotiating all outstanding geopolitical issues simultaneously as one 'Rubik's cube' — Taiwan, Cuba, Venezuela, Iran and Ukraine cannot be solved independently.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Investors should price geopolitical events as linked nodes in a single negotiation, not isolated shocks — a deal in one theater signals movement in others.
insights.quote: this is a kind of Rubik's cube approach where you say you can't fix any one of them independently of the others.

insights.claim: Malmgren frames the superpower choice as 'Star Wars vs Star Trek' — outright war is impossible given nuclear arsenals, so China is clearly signaling it prefers cooperation ('war with problems'), making de-escalation the base case.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Underpins her risk-on, recession-skeptic stance; a credible cooperative endgame caps the geopolitical risk premium in equities.
insights.quote: China is absolutely signaling that it prefers Star Trek. No question.

insights.claim: The implicit US-China deal: the US accepts Taiwan is in China's sphere and Cuba/Venezuela in America's, conditioned on China economically liberalizing toward a 'Taiwan model' and shifting from 'made in China' to 'owned by China' brands.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Implies reduced Taiwan-invasion tail risk and a US board vetting Chinese firms for market access — relevant for China brand/FDI and Taiwan-exposed semis.
insights.quote: China needs to move from a world where everything's stamped made in China to a world where they're stamped owned by China.

insights.claim: China flipped from Iran's sponsor to pressuring it because closing Hormuz hurt China most, so the US and China are now jointly setting reopening conditions — evidenced by the first transiting ships being Chinese oil vessels.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Chinese-flagged vessels getting priority transit is a tradable tell that a US-China side deal exists; watch vessel-flag composition of Hormuz traffic.
insights.quote: notice that the first ships that came through are all Chinese oil carrying vessels.

insights.claim: Malmgren claims Cuba is flipping: the CIA director flew to Havana the day Trump and Xi met, with the US 'dictating terms' (offering ~$100M cash to a fuel-starved Cuba) rather than doing diplomacy.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: A Cuba/Venezuela realignment into the US sphere would reshape Western Hemisphere energy and sanctions flows (e.g., Venezuelan crude to China).
insights.quote: Did you notice that the head of the CIA flew into Havana on the very day that Trump and Xi met?

insights.claim: Russia, like China, is open to a swap and will prioritize Ukraine over Iran, so Iran is treated as a 'done deal' and Ukraine is the last and hardest piece of the Rubik's cube to resolve.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Ukraine resolution is the key remaining catalyst; an Iran 'solution' may not arrive before Ukraine terms are set, keeping energy risk live.
insights.quote: if they have a choice between Iran and Ukraine, they're going to focus on Ukraine.

insights.claim: Malmgren says US strategy toward adversaries is 'conversion, not regime change' — creating conditions favorable enough that actors abandon terrorism for a place in the world economy, citing Syria's new leader's rehabilitation.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: A 'conversion' playbook (cash + market access) implies faster normalization of pariah states than military models — reduces long-run geopolitical risk premia.
insights.quote: The strategy is not regime change. The strategy is conversion.

themes.theme: Oil supply, demand destruction and price targets

insights.claim: Roughly 13 million bpd of Persian Gulf production is shut in and the US lacks the spare capacity to replace it, so ~10 million barrels of demand must be destroyed unless the strait reopens quickly.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend (host)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the supply gap; absent reopening, only sharply higher prices clear the market — bullish skew for crude.
insights.quote: we've got about 13 million barrels per day of production shutins in the Persian Gulf region as a result of the closing of the Straight of Hormuz.

insights.claim: Most analysts see $150-200 oil within ~30-60 days if Hormuz stays shut, as that's the price needed to destroy 10-15 million barrels of demand; world consumes ~105 mb/d vs ~90-95 mb/d supply capacity.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend (host) / Jim Bianco (guest)
insights.so_what: Defines the bull case price band and the structural supply-demand mismatch; the only durable fix Bianco names is more supply, not subsidies.
insights.quote: if this goes on for another month... most analysts are saying that puts us squarely at $150 to $200 oil

insights.claim: The world is currently bridging the gap by drawing down inventories (SPR and private), but Bianco says that runway ends around June/July, after which real demand destruction via higher prices becomes unavoidable.
insights.speaker: Jim Bianco (guest)
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete timing trigger (June/July inventory exhaustion) for when the oil price spike could begin in earnest.
insights.quote: oil inventories are being run down quite a bit... there's a finite limit... better measured as like June or July before we need to get resupplied.

insights.claim: Contrarian view from Dr. Anas Alhajji: demand destruction needed is only ~3 million barrels (not 10-15M), thanks to price-driven demand decline already underway and Saudi East-West pipeline workarounds via Bab-el-Mandeb.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend (host) citing Dr. Anas Alhajji
insights.so_what: Even the most bullish-on-supply analyst agrees prices rise if the closure persists — the debate is magnitude, not direction.
insights.quote: He thinks it's more like 3 million barrels... even if you take Honest's view, he's agreeing with me that if this goes on for another month, oil prices go up.

insights.claim: Bianco warns Trump's proposed gas-tax holiday and subsidies would backfire: handing consumers money to pay higher prices forces prices even higher to achieve the needed demand destruction.
insights.speaker: Jim Bianco (guest)
insights.so_what: Policy response itself could amplify the oil spike; a subsidy announcement is a contrarian bullish signal for crude, not bearish.
insights.quote: if we're going to try and subsidize high oil prices, we're just going to make it worse in the long run. The fix is more supply.

insights.claim: Patrick Ceresna stays a structural buyer of oil dips, arguing that even with a resolution, markets stay tight through an inventory-replenishment cycle and crude has room to trade back toward $100+.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna (co-host)
insights.so_what: Frames current ~10% selloff as a dip-buying opportunity; replenishment demand provides a floor under prices post-resolution.
insights.quote: oil has every opportunity to trade back toward 100 plus. So, I'm using this weakness to be buying dips.

themes.theme: Why markets are calm — and why that may be wrong

insights.claim: Bianco's behavioral thesis: markets refuse to react because investors who 'sold the bottom' on COVID, 9% inflation, SVB, and Liberation Day are now conditioned to dismiss every warning ('I'm not going to fall for that again').
insights.speaker: Jim Bianco (guest)
insights.so_what: Complacency is a recency-bias artifact; like 2007 subprime, the danger is markets staring down genuinely bad news until it abruptly catches up.
insights.quote: they were warned... and they sold the bottom of the market... Now they're being warned... And they're saying, Uh-huh. I'm not going to fall for that again.

insights.claim: Louis-Vincent Gave's brutal explanation (relayed by Townsend): the market isn't crashing because any mass starvation from the energy crisis would occur in countries that don't affect Nvidia's earnings; resources go to the highest bidder.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend (host) quoting Louis Gave
insights.so_what: Equity calm reflects that pain is externalized to poor importers; US/China-centric indices can stay elevated even amid a global energy shock.
insights.quote: if millions of people are going to starve to death as a result of this crisis, they won't be in countries that affect Nvidia's earnings.

insights.claim: Malmgren argues markets are calm because so many nations are aligned and moving toward resolution from an advanced (not from-scratch) starting point, so the leaderless Iranian holdouts cannot hold out long.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Bull case for risk assets rests on multilateral alignment; if that alignment visibly cracks, the complacency trade unwinds.

insights.claim: Bianco notes the dominant equity driver remains AI — including upcoming IPOs of SpaceX, Anthropic and possibly OpenAI — which will keep markets looking past the oil squeeze at least near-term.
insights.speaker: Jim Bianco (guest)
insights.so_what: The AI narrative is the structural bid; an oil shock competes with but does not yet displace it as the controlling market story.
insights.quote: the dominant theme in the stock market right now is still AI... the coming IPOs of SpaceX and anthropic and possibly open AI.

themes.theme: Modern warfare: cheap drones neutralize naval power

insights.claim: Bianco's core 'no forcing mechanism' thesis: cheap attritable drones have turned war upside down — Houthis closed Bab-el-Mandeb and defeated the French/British navies, and the same dynamic keeps Hormuz shut.
insights.speaker: Jim Bianco (guest)
insights.so_what: If naval force can't reopen the strait, the crisis lacks a military off-ramp — structurally bullish for oil duration risk and defense-tech investment.
insights.quote: these cheapraitable drones defeated the French and the British navy. We can't get the straight of Hermoose open.

insights.claim: Bianco's drone math: ~100 cheap drones beat a $3B US destroyer's 96 vertically-launched missiles because the last few always get through; this is why the US, UK, France, Russia and even China cannot force outcomes.
insights.speaker: Jim Bianco (guest)
insights.so_what: Implies China cannot reliably invade Taiwan and saturation-drone defense is the new arms race — bullish for counter-drone/cheap-munition defense names over legacy platforms.
insights.quote: I only need a hundred of them... the average US destroyer, which costs $3 billion, has 96 vertically launched missiles. And even if they're 100%, my last four get through.

insights.claim: Operation 'Protect Freedom' collapsed after two days: three US destroyers needed 100 aircraft flying 24/7 (F-35 at $100k/hour, ~$100M spent), two destroyers exhausted their missile magazines, and Gulf states revoked airbase access.
insights.speaker: Jim Bianco (guest)
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence the US cannot sustain a defensive shield over the strait — magazine depth and cost are the binding constraints favoring a prolonged closure.
insights.quote: two of them had to come back out because they ran out of missiles... that effectively ended Operation Protect Freedom after two days.

themes.theme: Molecules to atoms — the nuclear/energy transition trade

insights.claim: Malmgren argues the US is content to let Hormuz stay closed because it forces the world to buy US oil/gas and petroleum byproducts, and the US can sustain that role for about two years.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Reframes the US as a net beneficiary of the closure — bullish US energy exports and capex; explains White House non-panic.
insights.quote: the United States is perfectly fine with the straight remaining closed because this forces the whole world to buy these molecules... from America.

insights.claim: Malmgren cites the helium scare as proof supply chains shift fast: after IRGC hit Qatari plants, the world's largest helium reserve in Texas filled the gap and SK Hynix/Samsung signed Texas helium deals within ~3 weeks.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Supply-chain reroutability tempers shortage panics; US-located inputs (helium, energy) gain pricing power and volume during Gulf disruptions.
insights.quote: the largest helium reserve on Earth is in Texas. They're just going to buy from Texas.

insights.claim: Malmgren's structural thesis: the US is steering the world from 'molecules to atoms' — toward small modular reactors using TRISO fuel (poppy-seed pellets, meltdown-proof), with companies like Valar Atomics, Aalo and others in Austin.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Core long-term energy-transition trade: SMRs, advanced nuclear fuel and uranium fuel cycle as the structural beneficiary of the oil shock.
insights.quote: we now can put a star in a box literally... a box that's literally half the size of a car.

insights.claim: Malmgren claims an Austin startup built a small modular reactor with under 300 people in under 365 days, debunking the 'nuclear means $30B and 30 years' view — AI-driven simulation collapses development timelines.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: If nuclear build timelines compress dramatically, SMR/nuclear equities are mispriced to the upside; watch for accelerated regulatory approvals.
insights.quote: They have built a small modular reactor with less than 300 people in less than 365 days.

insights.claim: The 'Genesis Mission' — a White House initiative to declassify and run AI across all national-lab data (Los Alamos, Lawrence Livermore, Argonne) — is positioned to unleash world-shaking energy/tech breakthroughs.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: A policy catalyst to watch for breakthrough-tech commercialization (fusion, advanced energy); could re-rate energy-tech and AI-compute names.
insights.quote: We're going to run AI over all the data, and we're going to connect the dots within the labs and across the labs.

insights.claim: Ceresna's trade-of-the-week expresses the nuclear theme via a URA (Global X Uranium ETF) Jan-15-2027 bull call spread: buy $60 call (~$5.70), sell $70 call (~$3.50), ~$2.20 net debit, ~3.5:1 reward-to-risk, with URA near $49.58.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna (co-host)
insights.so_what: A concrete, defined-risk options structure to play the long-dated nuclear/uranium re-rating without absorbing full equity volatility.
insights.quote: buying the $60 call for about $5.70 and selling the $70 call for about $3.50, creating a $10 wide bull call spread for a net debit of roughly $2.20.

insights.claim: Malmgren says fertilizer fears are being neutralized by new petroleum-free methods like green ammonia, reinforcing that the oil shock is accelerating technological substitution away from hydrocarbons.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Substitution dampens the duration of commodity shortages; green-ammonia and non-hydrocarbon input tech are second-order beneficiaries of high oil.
insights.quote: we're developing all these new forms of things like green ammonia that don't even use any petroleum product.

themes.theme: AI, robotics and the cost-collapse economy

insights.claim: Malmgren's macro driver: debt burdens for the US, Europe, Britain and China are so massive that the only escape is a 'whole new level of production' via AI and robotics — distinct from incremental productivity.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Frames AI/robotics as a fiscal necessity, not just a tech cycle — implies sustained policy and capital support for the AI buildout regardless of valuation.
insights.quote: the debt burden for all the major players... is so massive that a regular human being who needs to sleep at night can't outrun this thing.

insights.claim: Malmgren reframes the AI-jobs panic as a confusion of 'jobs and tasks with income and standard of living' — robots doing tasks (like washing machines already do) frees humans for higher-value problem solving as supply costs collapse.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Contrarian to mass-unemployment fears; supports a deflationary-abundance thesis where cost of living falls faster than wages — bullish real consumption.
insights.quote: we're confusing jobs and tasks with income and standard of living and the disconnect is happening.

insights.claim: Malmgren points to ICON's 3D-printed homes in Austin (three-bed with Tesla Powerwall, selling power back to grid) as evidence housing+energy costs are collapsing toward a ~$100k home that 'pays its own mortgage.'
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Cost-collapse thesis spans housing and distributed energy; 3D-construction and distributed-solar/storage are structural disinflation plays.
insights.quote: your 3D printed home is paying the mortgage on your behalf.

insights.claim: Malmgren flags emerging energy frontiers — wireless energy transmission and space-based solar power (Japan recently made a breakthrough) — as paths to grid-independent, near-free power.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Early-stage but worth monitoring as optionality in the energy-abundance thesis; Japanese space-solar progress is a concrete watch item.
insights.quote: we're making big advances in some other new forms of energy like wireless transmission of energy, space-based solar power. Japan has just made a big breakthrough there.

insights.claim: Malmgren reaffirms she has argued since 2021 that 'we are in World War III' — beginning with Russia cutting Arctic subsea cables as a precursor to the Ukraine invasion — a global conflict only now being recognized and resolved.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: If the current conflict is the tail-end of a multi-year hybrid war, de-escalation (not escalation) is the forward path — supporting her risk-on stance.
insights.quote: I've been arguing since 2021... I think we are now in World War III... now I think it's beginning to be resolved.

insights.claim: Malmgren says 12-15 senior US nuclear/materials scientists (and younger Chinese scientists) have disappeared or died in statistically unlikely ways with little investigation, possibly tied to a fight over who controls soon-to-be-declassified nuclear tech.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Speculative but signals high-stakes contest over breakthrough energy/defense IP; watch national-lab declassification as a geopolitical and sector catalyst.
insights.quote: 12 and 15 of the most senior American... nuclear scientists... have literally just disappeared or suddenly been killed in very odd ways

insights.claim: Malmgren's contrarian patent angle: the UAP/UFO declassification may be a strategy to void private patents — under US law, technology of non-human origin can't be patented — stripping the military-industrial complex's IP monopoly.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Even as a 'ruse,' it's a framework for shifting tech IP into public domain and cutting defense costs — bearish for legacy-defense IP rents.
insights.quote: under US law, if the origin of a technology is not human, then the patents are not valid.

insights.claim: Malmgren ties the declassification push to Trump's broader cost-cutting strategy — telling defense firms to stop optimizing for buybacks/profits and pharma to stop charging Americans 3x global drug prices.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Signals margin/pricing-power compression risk for defense primes and pharma if the administration follows through — a sector headwind to monitor.
insights.quote: You guys have been optimizing for sure buybacks and for profits... this is going to end.

insights.claim: Malmgren positions herself as 'the most optimistic economist on the planet,' arguing the convergence of AI, robotics and new energy is 'bigger than the industrial revolution' and dismissing recession fears.
insights.speaker: Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)
insights.so_what: Sets the bullish macro frame against which the oil/geopolitical bears (Townsend, Bianco) argue — a useful barometer of the optimist case.
insights.quote: I may be the most optimistic economist on the planet at the moment.

themes.theme: Cross-asset positioning: FX, gold, rates, equities

insights.claim: Ceresna sees the US dollar (DXY ~99.50) set to challenge ~100.50 range-top, driven less by US strength than by euro/yen weakness as energy-import-dependent Japan and Europe eventually reprice the prolonged closure.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna / Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Watch EUR below 1.16 as the trigger; a sustained Hormuz closure is a bullish-USD, bearish-EUR/JPY energy-terms-of-trade trade.
insights.quote: I'm watching that euro breakdown below 116 as a... first kind of warning that... a dollar move may be underway.

insights.claim: Townsend expects gold (testing 200-DMA ~$4,400) to break lower toward the early-March $4,100 low and possibly below if oil spikes — a $150-200 oil scenario could even drag gold toward $3,000 before an eventual post-resolution bull market.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend (host)
insights.so_what: Counterintuitive near-term bearish gold call tied to oil/liquidity stress; the post-reopening setup is framed as a major gold buying opportunity.
insights.quote: I think we're very likely going to retest that $4,100 low from early March and maybe even take it out to the downside

insights.claim: Ceresna notes the entire precious-metals complex (gold, silver false breakout, platinum/palladium at lows, stressed miners) is showing distributive price action with no money flow — vulnerable to further downside until a mid-summer catalyst.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna (co-host)
insights.so_what: Broad PM-complex distribution argues against bottom-fishing now; flag July/August for a potential turn catalyst.
insights.quote: the entire precious metals market is showing that distribution... it's not being supported and that makes it relatively vulnerable for further distribution.

insights.claim: Bianco warns a new Fed chair (on the job under a week, last public speech a year ago, presumed dovish) cutting into a >4% CPI print risks a bond-market revolt — the market is pricing rate hikes, and stimulus could backfire by pushing yields higher.
insights.speaker: Jim Bianco (guest)
insights.so_what: If the White House forces cuts amid 4%+ inflation, the bond vigilante response (higher long yields) is the pain trade — bearish duration.
insights.quote: if the administration wants to scream Fed cut rates, the problem... is that the market is not there for the Fed to cut rates and it could actually backfire.

insights.claim: Ceresna highlights dangerously narrow equity breadth: the S&P at all-time highs (~7520) with only ~50% participation versus a typical 70-80%, making the AI/semiconductor-led rally vulnerable to either rotation or violent mean reversion.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna (co-host)
insights.so_what: Narrow breadth is the key risk to the rally; watch whether profit-taking in semis rotates into healthcare/defense/financials or triggers a broad selloff.
insights.quote: Rarely do we see a market that is trading at 52-week highs for such a prolonged period with the breath of the market not participating.

insights.claim: Ceresna keeps the 10-year Treasury yield (~4.51%) biased higher, arguing an unresolved Hormuz situation stresses oil, keeps inflation elevated, and likely pushes the 10-year back toward last month's highs.
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna (co-host)
insights.so_what: Oil-driven inflation keeps upward pressure on long yields; recent yield relief is likely short-lived absent an Iran resolution.
insights.quote: it's very likely here that we could see the 10-year yield just come right back up toward the highs from last month.
